title: Gate load-use stalls by actual source usage
motivation:
The current hazard unit treats IF/ID instruction bits [19:15] and [24:20]
as live source registers for every opcode. For OP-IMM, LOAD, JALR, LUI,
AUIPC, JAL, FENCE, EBREAK, and many illegal encodings, one or both fields
are immediate or unused bits. If those bits happen to match the destination
of a preceding load, the pipeline injects a needless bubble. CoreMark has a
dense mix of immediate arithmetic, loads, address updates, and control flow,
so removing these false load-use bubbles should improve iterations/cycle
without changing the pipeline depth or top-level contract.

dense mix of immediate arithmetic, loads, address updates, and control flow,
so removing these false load-use bubbles should improve iterations/cycle
without changing the pipeline depth or top-level contract.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a small opcode/funct3 based source-use predecode for the IF/ID
instruction and make the load-use detector compare the previous load's rd
only against source operands that the next instruction actually consumes.
R-type and branches use rs1+rs2, stores use rs1+rs2, OP-IMM/LOAD/JALR use
rs1 only, and LUI/AUIPC/JAL/MISC-MEM/SYSTEM use no data source for hazard
purposes. This preserves the required one-cycle stall for true load-use
cases while avoiding bubbles caused solely by immediate-bit aliasing.

title: Add fetch skid buffer
motivation:
The current 5-stage core directly couples the PC/IF output to decode and
inserts a bubble whenever io_imemReady is low. In the benchmark cosim mode,
instruction fetch sees about 22% random stalls, and downstream load-use or
dmem stalls also prevent useful fetch-ahead. A small instruction skid buffer
is a structural change that can hide some of that latency without changing
the single-retire RVFI contract.

dmem stalls also prevent useful fetch-ahead. A small instruction skid buffer
is a structural change that can hide some of that latency without changing
the single-retire RVFI contract.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Restructure IF into a decoupled fetch producer plus a one- or two-entry
valid/pc/instruction buffer feeding ID. When ID is stalled by load-use or
dmem backpressure and imem is ready, IF should continue advancing and enqueue
sequential instructions; when imemReady drops, ID can consume a buffered
instruction instead of immediately receiving a bubble. Redirects must flush
the buffer and restart at the redirect target. This should improve CoreMark
iterations/cycle under the apples-to-apples random iStall model while adding
only a few registers and muxes, with limited Fmax risk.

Wire the buffered IF status into the hazard logic and keep the nret=1 RVFI top-level interface unchanged.

title: Add small BTB branch predictor
motivation: The current 5-stage core always fetches sequentially and resolves branches and jumps in EX, so every taken loop branch pays a front-end redirect penalty. CoreMark has many tight backward loop branches, and under random instruction-memory stalls each wasted fetch cycle is especially expensive for iterations per cycle.

title: Gate false load-use stalls
motivation:
The pipeline currently stalls on any bit-pattern match between a load in
ID/EX and the raw rs1/rs2 fields of the following IF/ID instruction. Many
RV32I encodings do not actually consume both fields: OP-IMM and LOAD use
only rs1, LUI/AUIPC/JAL use neither, and SYSTEM/FENCE are source-free for
this core. These false dependencies inject bubbles in CoreMark's immediate
arithmetic, loop setup, and address-update code without improving
correctness.

hypothesis.hypothesis:

architectural register addresses and pre-write snapshots. This should recover

risk.

title: Resolve simple control flow in ID
motivation:
The current pipeline resolves every branch and jump in EX, so taken control
flow still carries a two-stage wrong-path penalty unless the BTB predicts it
correctly. The prior structural fetch skid buffer lost because it added IF
control and muxing without shortening a common dependency path. CoreMark has
many loop branches and unconditional jumps, so structurally moving simple
target/condition resolution one stage earlier can improve iterations/cycle
without widening retirement or adding front-end buffering.

title: Add tiny return address stack
motivation:
The existing direct-mapped BTB helps repeated fixed-target taken branches, but
it is weak for function returns because a single return instruction can have
different dynamic targets depending on the caller. CoreMark's small C working
set repeatedly calls shared helper routines, so return-target BTB aliasing can
still cause EX-stage redirects even after the BTB is warm.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a very small return address stack in the fetch stage and use lightweight
IF-side instruction predecode to predict JALR returns from x1/x5 using the
stack top. Update the stack only from valid EX-stage resolved control-flow:
push pc+4 for calls that write x1/x5 and pop for JALR returns, avoiding
speculative corruption while preserving precise redirect recovery. This should
complement the current BTB without increasing its associativity or broadening
the main BTB tag/target lookup, improving CoreMark IPC on call-heavy loops at
modest LUT cost.

title: Optimize load byte-lane formatter
motivation:
The current MEM stage formats byte and halfword loads with a variable
32-bit right shift by addr[1:0] * 8, then forwards the formatted value
directly into the EX-stage operand mux for load-dependent instructions.
That creates a likely critical path from dmem read data through a barrel
shifter, sign-extension mux, forwarding mux, and ALU/branch compare.
CoreMark has frequent loads and dependent address/data use, so improving
this path should raise FPGA Fmax without changing the single-retire IPC
model or any architectural behavior.

title: Move div/rem off EX critical path
motivation:
The current ALU keeps RV32M division and remainder as single-cycle
combinational operations in the EX stage. CoreMark's timed path is dominated
by loads, branches, integer ALU work, and multiplication, while DIV/REM are
rare or absent. Keeping a full divider on every EX-cycle timing path likely
costs Fmax and LUTs without improving CoreMark iterations per cycle.

title: Consolidate multiply datapath
motivation:
The current ALU builds three independent 64-bit products for MUL/MULH,
MULHU, and MULHSU on every EX cycle, then selects one result by opcode.

pressure and routing delay even when CoreMark mostly uses only a subset of
RV32M multiply forms. Prior attempts show the active win pattern is a local
critical-path/area reduction, not adding broad stall or front-end control.

title: Post ordinary RAM stores
motivation:
The current pipeline freezes the entire core whenever a store sits in MEM
and io_dmemReady is low. In the benchmark cosim mode dmem has random
backpressure, and CoreMark contains many stores whose results are not
immediately consumed by the next integer instructions. Prior structural
attempts regressed by lengthening the fetch/control critical path; this
targets a different bottleneck by decoupling store acceptance from the
rest of the in-order pipeline.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current pipeline freezes the entire core whenever a store sits in MEM
and io_dmemReady is low. In the benchmark cosim mode dmem has random
backpressure, and CoreMark contains many stores whose results are not
immediately consumed by the next integer instructions. Prior structural
attempts regressed by lengthening the fetch/control critical path; this
targets a different bottleneck by decoupling store acceptance from the
rest of the in-order pipeline.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a one-entry posted store buffer for aligned, non-MMIO RAM stores. When a
store reaches MEM while dmem is not ready, capture its address, replicated
write data, byte mask, and RVFI store fields, allow the store to retire once
it is safely enqueued, and continue executing independent non-memory
instructions while the buffer drains to dmem in the background. Preserve
architectural ordering by stalling any later load or store until the buffer
is empty, and never post UART or CoreMark timing-marker MMIO stores so the
start/stop bracket remains tied to real bus writes. This should improve

branch-resolution logic to the critical IF path.

title: Allocate BTB only on taken misses
motivation:
The current 16-entry direct-mapped BTB was the only predictor change that
improved CoreMark, but its gain is limited by aliasing. Every resolved
branch or jump currently installs a tag and target, even when a conditional
branch is not taken. A never-taken or mostly-not-taken conditional branch
predicts the same pc+4 path whether it misses in the BTB or hits a weak/strong
not-taken entry, so allocating those misses can evict useful taken loop and
call targets without improving the next-PC decision.

trains the 2-bit counter in both directions, but an update for a different
PC allocates/replaces the entry only when the resolved control-flow was
taken. On not-taken misses, leave the current entry untouched. This keeps
the IF lookup path PC-only and avoids the fetch-stage instruction decode
that hurt the JAL predictor attempt, while reducing direct-mapped BTB
pollution from forward conditionals and cold not-taken branches in CoreMark.

title: Drop regfile reset muxes
motivation:
The current integer register file is implemented as 32 resettable 32-bit
registers with two combinational read ports and write-first bypassing. That
forces a reset mux and high-fanout reset control onto every register bit,
and can prevent the FPGA tools from mapping the small 2R1W file into the
cheapest distributed storage. Recent wins came from local datapath and LUT
pressure reductions, while broader control-flow and store decoupling changes
regressed.

RISC-V software cannot depend on nonzero GPR reset contents, and the initial

design should lose 1024 reset muxes and a large reset fanout tree. This should
improve Tang Nano 20K Fmax and LUT pressure without changing CoreMark cycle
count or any memory/control-flow behavior.

title: Split execute and RVFI forwarding
motivation:
The current forwarding network uses the MEM-stage formatted writeback value for
both execute operands and RVFI source snapshots. That keeps the dmem read-data
formatting path connected to the EX operand muxes even though true load-use
consumers are already stalled until the load reaches MEM/WB. Prior wins show
that the dmem-to-forwarding path matters, while prior broken source-use work
shows RVFI raw source snapshots must still be preserved.

changing retirement width, load-use interlocks, memory ordering, or RVFI

title: Double PC-only BTB capacity
motivation:
The only successful predictor change so far was the small direct-mapped BTB,
and the lessons point to aliasing as the remaining limiter. Attempts that
added fetched-instruction predecode, return-stack speculation, or more update
policy control hurt timing or formal tractability. A capacity-only BTB change
keeps the predictor lookup PC-only while reducing conflicts among CoreMark's
repeated loop branches and calls.

behavior. This should reduce destructive aliasing without adding instruction
decode, extra ways, a RAS, or additional control-flow compares to the fetch
next-PC path. The expected benefit is mainly higher CoreMark iterations per
cycle from fewer warm-loop redirects, with modest Fmax risk from the slightly
larger indexed predictor arrays.

title: Register IF/ID boundary
motivation:
The bench core currently has a PC register in IF, but the IF/ID payload is
combinational: imem data, raw instruction fields, decoder outputs, immediate
generation, and regfile read addresses all feed the ID/EX register in one
cycle. Prior structural attempts that added predictors, buffers, or early
control logic regressed because they lengthened IF-side muxing or broad
pipeline control. A narrower structural retiming can attack the same frontend
timing pressure without adding a larger predictor or side buffer.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Convert IF/ID into a true registered pipeline boundary that captures pc,
predicted pc, instruction, and valid when imem is ready, holds across ID or
dmem stalls, and flushes on redirects. This cuts the imem-data-to-decode and

registered entry can also let ID consume one already-fetched instruction when
the next imem access stalls. The cost is one extra frontend stage and a larger
redirect bubble on unpredicted control flow, but CoreMark's hot loop branches
are already helped by the existing BTB, so the expected net gain is higher
Fmax plus modest iStall tolerance with much less control complexity than the
earlier skid-buffer attempt.

title: Narrow BTB tags
motivation:
The current successful predictor is a 16-entry direct-mapped BTB/BHT, but
every IF cycle still performs a full 32-bit PC tag comparison before choosing
the predicted target. Prior predictor attempts show that larger tables,
fetched-instruction predecode, taken-only allocation policy, and RAS state add
more front-end/control cost than CoreMark repays. CoreMark's timed image is a
small 6 KB working set in low memory, so a full 32-bit tag is unnecessary for
distinguishing useful in-image control-flow PCs.

dependency even though MEM-stage forwarding already formats load data and can

sequences where removing those bubbles should improve iterations per cycle.

title: Simplify BTB direction state
motivation:
The useful predictor changes so far point toward preserving the small PC-only

return-stack state, or more entries. The current IF path still reads a 2-bit
counter per entry and gates the hit target through the counter MSB, while the
update path contains saturating increment/decrement logic for every resolved
control-flow instruction. CoreMark's hot backward branches are mostly taken and
already recover from loop exits by keeping the target resident, so a simpler
direction representation may trade a small amount of hysteresis for better Fmax.

title: Bypass ALU for ADD results
motivation:
The latest best result came from keeping CoreMark's load/store effective
addresses and jump link values off the monolithic RV32IM ALU result path.
After that change, ordinary ADD/ADDI arithmetic and AUIPC address formation
still route through the full ALU case tree, including the wide M-extension
result muxing, even though these hot operations only need a 32-bit adder.
CoreMark contains frequent pointer increments, loop counters, and pc-relative
address materialization, so shortening the ADD-family writeback/forward path
is a promising local micro-optimization without changing IPC behavior.

motivation:

already encoded in the instruction funct3 field that the pipeline carries

width and local mux pressure, while recent regressions came from adding new
hot fast-lane selects or reusing heavily fanned-out controls.

title: Narrow post-EX control payload
motivation:
The current pipeline still carries the full decoded ctrl_t through EX/MEM and
MEM/WB even though most of those bits are dead after execute. MEM only needs
memory access shape, writeback select, reg_write, and trap state, while WB and
RVFI only need reg_write, mem_to_reg, and is_illegal. Prior successful work
showed that pruning real inter-stage payload and fanout can improve Tang Nano
20K fitness, while broader structural changes that add buffering or new stall
control tend to regress.

Matching resident entries should train exactly as today; invalid slots should allocate on any resolved control-flow update, including not-taken conditionals, so cold branches can still be learned; valid mismatched slots should be replaced only by taken updates, leaving the resident entry intact on not-taken alias misses. This preserves broad feedback when capacity is available while avoiding the most damaging not-taken pollution of hot CoreMark loop targets, with no added IF-side decode or lookup state.
